top_profane.text:
Trash cans haven’t always been around for families to dispose of their trash in. So what did they do with their garbage? That depended on where they lived.

Farmers and their families turned food scraps into pig slop. Of course, pigs will eat anything, even dead bodies, so who knows what was fed to them. What wasn’t fed to the pigs was buried in a hole in the ground or burned. So for those who lived on farms, trash disposal wasn’t that much of an issue.

But what about those who lived in the cities, especially those in apartment buildings? They wouldn’t own their own property to dig holes and bury it. Tenants might own pets, but it is doubtful they would have their own pigs. And burning trash inside an apartment probably wouldn’t be a good idea if you wanted to keep your apartment.

What options were left? Residents didn’t want the trash to pile up inside their home, so they simply threw it out the window and onto the street. If there was already too much garbage in the street—it wouldn’t take long for it to accumulate—it could be hauled to a river or some other body of water and dumped. Thinking about the stench all of the trash plus the fact that horses would urinate and defecate on these same streets makes me thankful for trash cans, garbage men, and the system of waste disposal we now have. And it also makes me thankful that everyone doesn’t travel by horse.

Pigs will eat anything. A lot of small towns and cities had pigs set loose on the streets so they would eat the trash. That would help the trash problem, but pigs would also leave behind their own kind of trash.

All of the filth in the streets only led to disease. Something had to be done. The streets had to be cleaned by something other than pigs and vultures. Trash didn’t need to be thrown there in the first place.

Laws to keep trash from being dumped in the streets were passed and ignore. In 1757, Ben Franklin started the first street cleaning service, and the public was encouraged to dig pits—where I don’t know—to dispose of their waste.

In 1875 England, the first trash bin (trash can) appeared and weekly garbage collection began. Somewhere between 1885 and 1900, trash cans also appeared in America.

The trash can is one item that I’ve always taken for granted, and it is a necessity. You need at least two of them in your house: one for the kitchen and one for the bathroom.

Trash cans can be bought just about anywhere. And to be honest, I never thought about how important a trash can was until my recent purchase of a new one. I was used to the trash bags breaking if the trash in them weighed too much. And every so often, of course, I ended up with a defective trash bag that would bust and send my garbage spilling across the bottom of my trash can and across my kitchen floor. Because of this, the trash can never stayed completely clean. Never. And that didn’t please me.

But the simplehuman trash can I had bought and put in my bathroom was perfect. My simple Dollar Store trash can needed to be replaced with one that my dog couldn’t get into. Precious was the perfect dog, but when she was mad at me, her one form of rebellion was to go and pull things out of the bathroom trash can. That had to stop. So I bought a trash can that had a lid on it, a lid that could only be opened by stepping on a pedal. Perfect. Precious never did figure out how to open it.

The simplehuman trash can I chose for my kitchen is almost too pretty to be a trash can. When you step on its wide steel pedal, the lid soundlessly opens. When your foot moves off the pedal, it closes just as quietly. But a trash can without a trash bag wouldn’t be much use.

The trash bags (liners) that simplehuman makes for what has to be the ultimate trash can are super strong; they make all other trash bags seem flimsy in comparison. Have you ever had your trash bag slide off the lip of the can and fall down into the yucky trash? The simplehuman trash bags fit so securely there is no chance of this happening.

Any trash can would be an improvement over having to throw your trash onto the street or into a body of water, but this simplehuman trash can will spoil you.
top_profane.title: Talking Trash

top_profane.text:
We are all familiar with the visual of the crotchety, old man shouting at cars driving 3 mph over the speed limit in his neighborhood. When we see him we may think to ourselves, "Geeze guy, settle down, you're going to pull something..." before it dawns on us that before long, WE will be that crotchety old man or woman. For me anyway, there is this realization that given how jaded I already am at 35 about all of life's little annoyances that full-on crochetyness is an inevitability...and that it isn't all that far off.

What makes me particularly salty are all the CRAP products out there that CRAP OUT on you before they should. When did it become a bleeping foregone conclusion that 80% of consumer products will turn to worthless CRAP in mere months? $200 for this? CRAP in a heartbeat. $120 for that? Yep, turned to CRAP in lightning speed. Sure, the more you spend the lesser the probability the product will be CRAP but there is no hard and fast rule where "you spend X and you're guaranteed to avoid CRAP." It's a poor commentary on the whole human race in my opinion...but I digress.

Every so often however, in the midst of thousands upon thousands of CRAP products here and CRAP products there, you come across a gem. THIS BLENDER, LADIES AND GENTLEMAN, IS THAT GEM. I think I paid $29 for this thing when I bought it a few months ago. I figured meh...I can either spend $29 and get a CRAP blender or $100 and get CRAP blender like I've historically done in the past...so I decided to go the cheap CRAP option. You can imagine my surprise and utter delight when this beast turned out to NOT be CRAP...but a ray of light and hope in a dark, dark CRAP consumer-product world.

I use this blender virtually every night and I'm not easy on it. I jam a lot of frozen fruit down it's gullet before slamming the puree button without remorse and it gobbles the frozen fruit like Joey Chestnut gobbles hotdogs on the Fourth of July. It's incredible. I love this blender. I LOVE IT. And to have paid $29 bucks for this thing?! You kiddin me?! Unreal!

Despite relying upon reviews, I seldom write them because I'm a busy guy...and that is something I feel badly about. But this freaking blender... I couldn't NOT write a review. So I hope it helps you make the decision to buy this thing...and I hope when you buy it and start loving it like I do...that it helps you to become just a little less jaded too.

March 2022 Update - Oh yes, she's still goin' strong! So if you came here looking for CRAP, best keep a walkin'!
top_profane.title: I'm stunned...

top_profane.text:
This item is one of those where you wonder whether it is worth the price, and we are still wondering. Consider that trash cans are available from the local discount store, or even Amazon, for a tenth of the price of this trash can, or less, and the magnitude of this purchase is suddenly realized. Even so, this trash can is definitely the most sophisticated and beautiful trash can we have ever owned.

The best, and worst, part about this trash can is the automatic opening feature. The trash can opens in two ways. First, there is a sensor that detects movement of a hand (and other body parts) near the trash can, which initiates opening of the trash can. Second, this trash also opens with a voice commend. However, sometimes the trash can randomly opens by itself. So, is it some kind of issue with the sensor, or is the voice command detecting sound and interpreting it as the "open can" command? We are still experimenting, but have been unable to figure it out - so far. Regardless, when the can opens somewhat randomly it can be disconcerting.

One cool part of the automatic opening is technology behind the opening. The motor is relatively low torque, but a planetary gear reduction system provides plenty of power to open and close the lid, with minimal sound. We wonder a bit about the lifetime of the gears, but we anticipate years of life unless someone slams the lid down or does something else that would impede operation of the gear reduction.

Beyond the automatic opening feature, this can is perfect for a stainless steel kitchen. The finish on this stainless steel can is gorgeous and seems resistant to fingerprints. As long as we treat this can as we do our other stainless steel appliances, the finish should last for a lifetime.

Each of the two containers, one of which includes a removable plastic container, is suitable for standard 13 gallon kitchen bags, though the 13 gallon bags are a bit larger than the containers of this trash can. The company that makes this trash can also makes trash bags that are a good fit with this trash can. We wonder if they deliberately made the trash can off-sized just to offer better fitting trash bags. Another cool feature of this trash can is the internal compartment that holds trash bags, though we still seem to pull another bag from the enormous roll we buy from the local discount club store rather than loading the compartment.

While this trash can has a lot going for it, the price is a bit steep. On the other hand, it is technologically cool. Our thought is that most people are unlikely to put the cash out there to buy a fancy, automatically-opening trash can because of the cost. However, if the automatic feature is desirable, this trash can certainly seems like a contender, if we can keep it from opening all by itself.

This trash can receives four stars for two reasons.

First, there is the price. Yes, the trash can is stainless steel, which is a relatively expensive metal. Yes, the gearing, sensors, and associated electronics are relatively expensive, but provide one of the coolest features of the trash can. And, yes, it is a dual trash can that can handle recycling as well as non-recyclables. So, for the price, there is a lot of value, but is that value sufficient to convince people to replace their $10-$20 trash can with something significantly more expensive. We wonder.

Second, the periodic random opening of the lid. While initially amusing, the random opening quickly becomes annoying. We are considering taping over sensors (if possible) to try to determine which sensor is the source of the random opening. If we can figure out which sensor likes to operate with minimal prompting, we may leave that sensor permanently disabled, or try to figure out how to reduce sensitivity of that particular sensor.

If the periodic random opening problem were solved, we might be more tempted to make this product a five-star product, but maybe not, because the price is significant. If the price were reduced significantly AND the random opening issue were solved, than this product would be an absolute winner.

Enjoy!
top_profane.title: Looks great and opens automatically, though sometimes randomly

top_profane.text:
After other residents in my building reported seeing rats and cockroaches, I decided to invest in a lidded kitchen trash can to make my unit less attractive to any would-be pests.

Because I only use grocery bags as trash bags, I realized that 12L was the highest-capacity trash can that I could get that would work with grocery bags. After thoroughly researching the available options (and even checking at my local Target, Walmart, Homegoods, and TJ Maxx stores), I decided to get this Amazonbasics trash can.

Now that I've used it for over a week, I am confident that I picked the best-possible 12L trash can on the market. Here's why:

1. Step-on is best: Step-on is easier and quicker to use when disposing the bits of veggies and fruits that are inedible as one is prepping ingredients to cook--a motion-activated lid would have required interacting with the trash for longer and with more attention than a step-on lid. I don't even have to look at the trash can to use it when I'm cooking. I just keep it next to me and step on it whenever I need to throw out peels, and can keep chopping in the meantime.

2. Quality pedal mechanism: I tried other step-on trash cans with flimsy pedal mechanisms that broke after a few uses. This one is well-made and should last for years.

3. Rectangular shape: A rectangular shape is best when using grocery bags as trash bags, as I am, if one wants to maximize the amount of trash that can fit into a standard grocery bag's dimensions. If I were to use a round trash can with a standard grocery bag, the trash can would compress the bag and reduce the amount of trash that it could hold before the can fills up.

4. Horizontal-opening lid: most rectangular trash cans of this size have lids that hinge on the shorter side of the trash can. Because more of the trash bag is exposed if the lid hinges horizontally than if it hinged on short end, I don't have to worry about carefully aiming my trash at the bag in order to prevent it from splattering on the lid.

5. Removable plastic bin: This part holds the grocery bags perfectly, and can be easily cleaned if a bag leaks or has holes. It also is lifted up when the lid opens.

6. Soft-close lid: No one wants to hear a banging trash lid!

7. Quality stainless steel exterior: I saw that the simplehuman 12L trash can had complaints about flimsy metal and about some trash cans arriving dented. This one arrived in perfect condition and seems to be thicker than many other trash cans I've used. It also looks good and is easy to clean.

8. Trash can doesn't "walk away": Just like it says, it's weighted enough that it doesn't slide on a smooth tile floor when you push the pedal, even when it's empty.

9. Price: Most 12L trash cans cost $30-50 and lack the pros mentioned above. This one only cost me about $20. In my eyes, that's a bargain, especifically once you factor in the quality.

All-in-all, I love this little trash can, and I'd recommend it to anyone that wants to use grocery bags as trash bags. If for some reason I should lose or damage this trash can in the future, I'm definitely going to be repurchasing it instead of buying from a different brand.
top_profane.title: The Best Soft-Close 12L Trash Can

top_profane.text:
Yes, I did. I did get a 200 hundred dollar garbage can. Shaking my head, even now. The reason? I hate garbage cans.

I hate them. I hate looking at them. I hate them in the kitchen, the bathroom, the study, any room at all. And I especially hate them in other people's homes. I have an in law who has the most gross plastic white garbage can situated promptly in the middle of the kitchen at the end of her counter, which is in the middle of the whole house. Gross. Yes, it does have a lid. But still, gross. She is from another time, possibly another dimension, so it probably would never occur to her that a large plastic can of old food and grossness in the middle of your house (open concept house here) and middle of food area and middle of people gathering would ever be in the slightest of distaste, for she would of course need taste in the first place - ouch... Anyway, same for my father - same garbage displays and grossness and possible other dimension.

So because of my issues with inside garbage cans (mine or others), no matter where I have moved, I always put them under the kitchen sink. I buy tiny garbage cans - one for recycle also - and take the garbage out every day. They are open cans, but since I always owned my own homes in the past, there were no problems. Until recently.

Recently I moved into a rental..oops..and there were bugs...double oops. So I spray and put out traps and control them, I think. I'm no bug person, so I thought all was taken care of. But then they came back, with a vengeance. My landlord keeps apologizing. There were dead bugs when I moved in, but he said they were nothing and I had already made it to where I couldn't change addresses..triple oops. So, rental, bugs, kill them, bugs again, finally have to get rid of my garbage containers under the sink. Damn. I hate seen garbage cans. Garbage cans should be hidden. Now I have to have 2 seen garbage cans, because I also recycle. 2 cans displayed in my kitchen. With no where to hide them. Damn.

Upon researching many hours, because I hate seen garbage cans but I hate bugs in my kitchen even more, I keep tripping over the simplehuman garbage/recycling container that looks beautiful for 200 bucks. I can't do it but I must.

So instead of purchasing it myself, I ask my father to buy it for me for my upcoming birthday present, but with the added condition that he must also buy one for himself. He agrees, to my astonishment. Why hadn't I tried this before with other things?

The 200 hundred dollar garbage/recycling containers = they are both beautiful, mine and his. They sit in the eating areas of both of our places and beckon you to put your garbage into them and they will discreetly yet lovingly protect you from your garbage and recycling. Yes, this is what these works of garbage container art do. They protect you from your garbage and the seen garbage cans, because, really, what is that? Is that your garbage? Can that really be a garbage can? No, it is an artful container that keeps your kitchen tidy and hides life's little unpleasantnesses like gross garbage cans away from your eyes or as uppity millenniums or "adults" playing at work in universities would say, "first world problems." So if you have a first world problem like garbage can revulsion, this is definitely your can.
top_profane.title: This is definitely your can

top_profane.text:
Don't you hate it when you have your hands full and you are trying to empty something into the trash or your hands are greasy and you have to come back and CLEAN the trash can lid after you use it? Yea, me too. Enter the "simplehuman Semi-Round Trash Can". With just a wave of the hand, the lid quickly opens wide and you can easily dump your trash in with little fuss and bother. No need to push a pedal with your foot or find and push a button on the lid (and come back and clean it later) in order to get the lid to open on it's own. The sensor is very responsive and does not make the lid open every time someone is remotely near it. Both times I made the lid open when I didn't want to, I was standing directly in front of the can. The Simplehuman can also uses any 12 gallon trash bags so you can easily transition from your old, dumb can to the "simplehuman Sensor Trash Can".

Another pet peeve that this trash can gets rid of is that typical trash cans have closed bottoms. So when you put in a new bag, the static electricity in the bag and the air pressure in the bottom of the can prevents the bag from opening up all the way. Thus you need to wait until there is some trash in the bag, then come back and remove the bag from the lid, let the air come out of the can, and then reattach the bag to the trash can. Yes, it's a minor nitpick, but you change the trash bag several times a week and it gets old having to mess with it twice every time. Since the "simplehum Sensor Trash Can" has no bottom, the bag billows out on it's own - problem solved!

One comment I have on the trash bags is that it appears that simplehuman has their own bags ([[ASIN:B0015RAHUU simplehuman Custom Fit Trash Can Liner M, 45 Liters / 12 Gallons, 50-Count]]) that are "Extra durable plastic (with) thick double seams (that) prevent rips and tears". I assume that this is in response to the trash can having no bottom and fears that buyers may have of trash leaking on their floors. I've had the can for a month and never given a trash bag rip a second thought - I buy the generic store brand. Of course we don't overload the bag and we bag sharp things separately, but I think you'd have to be crazy to spend $18 on simplehuman's 50-count trash bags when the $2 store brand works just as good.

Overall I thought that the "simplehuman Sensor Trash Can" worked like a dream and if I could review it higher than 5-stars I would!

************UPDATE************

Although the six C-Cell batteries that were included in the packaging for the trash can only lasted about six weeks (product info states that they should last six months), the next set of batteries that I put in actually lasted just over six months so my previous impression that the trash can burnt through batteries appears have been premature. I therefore changed my review back to 5 Stars.

********ANOTHER UPDATE********

Changed out the batteries at the 10 month point (so this is the third time I've changed SIX C-CELL batteries. Note - they are supposed to last 6 months). 1 week later, the motor died. Absolutely dead - I even saw a couple little sparks when I took it apart. So I'm unhappy about the money I've spent on batteries and NOW that a trash can that is supposed to last FIVE YEARS is dead. I mean I got it free to try from Amazon, but still... We had to dig the old trash can out of the garage so I put my review back to 3 stars - grrrr...

*********FINAL UPDATE*********

Received a free warranty replacement motor from Simplehuman. The replacement motor was VERY easy to install - took me about 5 min from start to finish. So at this point, the trashcan is obviously working as good as new. So maybe the issues that I had been seeing with the trashcan eating batteries was simply the result of a malfunctioning motor. Because of Simplehumans easy warranty replacement process and because the trash can appears to be working as good as new again, I've upped my review to 4 stars. Hope it was just a bad motor because I really like the trash can!
top_profane.title: Absolutely Love The Trash Can - BUT - I Experienced Battery/Electrical Issues

top_profane.text:
I've been a huge fan of Simple Human products for years. They are best known for their trash cans, but they make so many great household items that we use in our home. Shower caddies, soap dispensers, makeup mirrors, and even toilet plungers! All fantastic. Their products tend to cost a bit more, but they're always high quality and extremely durable. This butterfly trash can is no exception.

I decided to purchase this particular trash can because we have such limited space in our kitchen. We needed something narrow with a lid, but the lid had to also be compact. Most trash cans with hinged lids have the hinge part sticking out of the back. Not only do those hinges take up space, it creates a gap that prevents the trash can from sitting perfectly flush against a wall.

Given our very narrow and restrictive space, I knew finding a trash can that would fit could prove difficult. I also knew I may not have the luxury of choosing a specific brand, because the dimensions were more important. In the end this butterfly trash can seemed to be the only one that would work. However, the price is WAY higher than I have ever paid for a receptacle that holds my garbage. Even now I feel sick just thinking about what I paid for it. But at least it works and fits where I needed it to go.

It wasn't just that this trash can fit the space, it is also the butterfly lid design that makes it even more perfectly suited for tight and narrow spaces. We have a couple of the smaller butterfly trash cans in our bathrooms, which I love, and it really is a genius design. Because the lid will never exceed the dimensions of the trash can itself, except for the height obviously. So it's not just a cool-looking gimmick; it actually serves a useful purpose, especially if you're working with limited space. I also really appreciate the removable insert that holds the trash. This makes it really easy to change the bags, once again, when you have limited space or access to maneuver the entire receptacle without it being a giant pain in the you-know-what.

So yeah, it's a great trash can. I'm not sure that any trash can justifies this kind of price, but it certainly does a good job of holding my garbage. Having said that, I paid a 5-star price for a 4-star trash can (in my opinion). It's ALMOST perfect, but not quite. I have two relatively minor complaints about it. First, I wish it was a standard 13 gallon size like our old trash can. Knowing that we would fill a 13 gallon in one week, I was initially concerned that we'd have to replace the bags more often. Luckily that turned out not to be the case, but only BARELY. Seriously, it's enough for 7 days for the two of us, but it would not make it to 8 days. But there is still a bit of room in the trash bag once removed to fit in some extra items before tossing it out should it be necessary.

My second complaint, again relatively minor, has to do with the coolest feature: the butterfly lid. Our previous trash can, also by Simple Human and with a hinged lid, had the option to keep the lid open if you wanted. Having that option was useful for a couple of reasons. First, it prevents garbage from touching the lid once it starts to get pretty full. I use this brand's liners which aren't cheap, so I want to make sure those bags are FULL. The second reason having the lid stay open is it allows you to air it out. Closed lids are great for keeping trash concealed and containing smells, but those smells hit you in the face the second that lid opens. And quite often those smells linger inside the can even after removing the trash. So I clean it often, and like to have the lid stay open after I clean it so that everything's dry and fresh again. But the only way to keep the lid open on this is to press the foot pedal. This means I need to find some object that's heavy enough to sit on the pedal, because obviously I'm not going to stand on it all day. Other than those two things, and the price, it's a great trash can.
top_profane.title: Almost Perfect

top_profane.text:
I ordered 5, 3.5-inch paring knives to be able to compare their performance side by side. Every kitchen needs good paring knives. I tested them on onions, sweet potatoes, apples, carrots and paper. Here are the results:
1. ZHEN VG-10 67-layer Damascus Steel, weight 94 g. Blade thickness - just larger than 1mm. Cuts very nicely through paper smoothly and without resistance. This knives shines through in tough vegetables like sweet potatoes and carrots. The extremely stiff blade aids in this test. If just feels powerful as it slices through hard things. It also glides through onions very well. Bolster shape allows for the entire blade to be sharpened. I liked the heft, but my wife did not like the handle thickness. So I guess this might not be the best knife if you have Trump-sized hands. Finally, sharpening. This is harder steel, so it shouldn't need sharpened as regularly as a German steel knife, but I have heard that VG-10 can be more time consuming to sharpen. I'm not a steel expert though so correct me if I'm wrong.
2. F.Dick 1905 series. weight 69g. Blade thickness seems about the same as the ZHEN knife (just larger than 1mm). WHY ARE F.DICK KNIVES NOT BETTER KNOWN??? This knife is close tie with the ZHEN knife. It is a different style knife (so not exactly comparing apples to apples here), with German steel instead of Japanese steel. The slicing ability is comparable to the ZHEN knife. The distal taper on the blade is particularly clever and makes for better cutting than the Wusthof classic. It doesn't feel as stiff as ZHEN when you cut with it, but it still makes easy work of cutting food in a different way. It seems to go through paper a little smoother than the ZHEN knife. This knife is my wife's favorite because the handle is much more comfortable for her (it's comfy for me too). The weight of the knife if perfect. It is not too heavy and it is not too light.
3. Wusthof Classic. weighs 63g. Thickness seems the same as the ZHEN and F.Dick knives. This is America's Test Kitchen winning paring knife (ZHEN and F.Dick were not in their lineup). I have used it for years. I thought it was the best until I tried the F.Dick (from what I can tell, the distal taper makes F.Dick better than Wusthof). The other design issue is that the bolster gets in the way of sharpening this knife.
4. Victorinox Fibrox Pro. 17g. Blade thickness about 1mm. This is America's Test Kitchen winning best buy. I have also used this for years. It is a good little knife and for less than $10 it is a great deal. But this knife struggles because the blade is too flexible. It seems to bend out of the way for tough foods. This knife is good for detail work on something like an apple or softer. ZHEN and F.Dick seem to slice through easier and cleaner though. For me, the biggest issue is the weight. It is just too light. Featherweight. To some, this would be a good thing. I don't like it though. Makes it feel cheap. The bolster gets in the way of sharpening the entire blade.
5. Dalstrong Shogun Series. 102g! This VG-10 Japanese steel blade is a tank at 2mm thick! It is not a good thing either. Cutting everything was more difficult compared to all of the other knives in the lineup because it acted as a wedge between the food. It is bulky and clumsy. I would not recommend this knife in comparison with the others. Aside from all of that, it was the most expensive.

UPDATE: tonight I was peeling and cutting a very hard butternut squash with this knife. I compared it to the F.Dick. The ZHEN outshines the F.Dick hands down. For things softer you can't tell much of a difference, but for hard veggies this is the very clear winner. Holy smokes this thing makes prep work easy!
top_profane.title: ZHEN wins!

top_profane.text:
I bought this for my parents as a Christmas present. It replaced a terrible 13 gallon plastic trash can. The old trash can had one of those swing doors, and as that door swung back and forth it would waft old food and garbage smell in the kitchen. YUCK.

This. Is. No. Ordinary. Trash can.

This trash can is sleek and overall looks very cool. Pictures are just as advertised. Even though it’s a metal trash can, it’s not super heavy.

Since my parents have cats who like to chew cords, I installed D batteries into the trash can and they haven’t shown any signs of losing power. The door opens when you simply hold your trash in front of it, and then you just drop it in and the trash can closes up, sealing away stinky smells and unsightly gross garbage. This trash can is built to seal stink inside!

Not to mention, it has WHEELS which I was originally against cause I thought it would look weird. It looks fine, the wheels are very small and also have a tiny brake you can tap down with your foot so you can park it where you need it. The wheels don’t work very well on high carpet, but on flat floors this trash can can be pulled around to where you need it. Often I will slide it to my side in the kitchen while I peel carrots and potatoes. If you stand directly in front of the trash can, it will just remain open as long as you need, so I can take my time to peel each vegetable with care and it’ll close when I’m done.

I use cheap $1.00 trash bags from Dollar Tree and this trash can is just fine with that. The trash can looks slim but it’s built to ensure every nook and cranny of the bag inside will be full of trash.

Downsides: My only complaint is trash bag removal. It’s very easy to lift off the top, but after shoving a lot of trash down into the bag it will create a suction and the only way to get the trash out is by pulling in a back and forth motion from the top of the bag. My husband said this unfortunate suction seal issue could easily be fixed by just drilling a small hole into the bottom of the trash can. But of course, if you like to throw away wet stuff, a hole in the bottom might not be ideal. You’d probably want to buy some stronger trash bags to battle the suction.

I will be purchasing one of these for myself in the near future.
top_profane.title: Super convenient

top_profane.text:
Plastic lid broke on my SimpleHuman 38L/10 Gal trash can after 15 years of use. I used regular store bought bags for it, they used to fall inside from time to time. Kohler trash can's inner bin is 1 inch shorter and 2 inches wider then SimpleHuman's, so store bought trash bags fit perfect.
Now, negative. If you look at the picture in the description of the product, counter tops on that cabinets are flash with the cabinet wall and trash can sits snugly against it. My granite sticks out about 2 inches, and every time I open the trash can lid, it hits against the granite. It creates a problem to remove trash bag, too. To do it, one needs to lock lid and rim in vertical position, but there is a granite counter top over there. So, I had to move trash 3 inches away from the cabinet. I did not have this problem with SimpleHuman trash, because their lid in open position is 2 inches shorter then Kohler trash can lid and fits right under counter top. Also, SimpleHuman lid does not have to be strictly vertical to remove trash bag. It is difficult to move Kohler trash can because it heavy and has no handle, like SimpleHuman trash can has on the back.
To open SimpleHuman trash can, I can push pedal down with a tip of my toes, but with this trash can I have to apply significant pressure with my foot. Might be, it is good for dog owners, but muscles on my foot already hurt after doing it several times in the row, while cleaning the kitchen. Children hate the stiff pedal. Every time someone opens the trash can, rim makes loud banging noise, scaring cats and humans. SimpleHuman trash cans, including new models with rim, are silent.
Meanwhile, Tramontina trash can arrived, 13 Gal, black. It is definitely of lower quality, compare to SimpleHuman and Kohler. Positive: It is easy to open, does not bang. Negative futures. Trash bag does not fit as snugly as Kohler. It can not seat flash against the wall or counter top because it has bulging part on the back, about 2 inches. Lid height is the same as Kohler's. The main problem: it is very shiny, like a black mirror. I have black granite counter tops and this trash can outshines them by far. All the fingerprints show up. The only way to remove them is with stainless steel cleaner, that makes trash bin even more shiny. Windex does not work.
We kept Kohler trash can. I attached sticky felt pads that are used for furniture legs to my counter tops to prevent banging against them. Also, I cut clear gel like dots, normally used under table glass tops, to fit under the trash can rim. So far so good, but I do not think they will stay there for long.
top_profane.title: Fits "snugly against the wall", but not against kitchen cabinets with granite.

top_profane.text: I originally bought this as a meat thermometer after I started cooking mini home meals for myself. I live in a second floor apartment, and my oven is a piece of crap, so instead of getting undercooked chicken because I was relying on my crappy oven, I bought this and decided to let science tell me when my chicken was ready. It's easy to set up, and it has a magnet attached to it, so you can stick it to the side of your crappy oven too. I love this damn thing. About a year ago I was in a motorcycle accident. It crushed my foot and I lost a lot of blood circulation in my foot. The doctors told me to ice it and rest it, which of course I did. They didn't tell me that after you ice it you should also apply heat, so it took about 8 months to heal. At some point I got so tired of being crippled that I bought Epsom salt from Amazon and started giving myself Epsom salt baths. Then I read online that between 100° f and 112° f were the best temperatures for a heated bath. I just so happened to have the therm Pro meat thermometer that I had bought for my crappy oven. It was then that I smiled, because I knew that it would also work for my crappy foot. The damn thing works great and I love it. 2 days ago I was moving things around and it fell out of my hand into the vat of the Epsom salt. I immediately retrieved it, remove the battery, then laid it on a towel. I had written the damn thing off until just now, when after trying another crappy thermometer I decided to turn this thing on for shits and giggles. The damn thing still works, and I love it. This trashcan is the best trashcan I've ever had. I'm going to echo the other reviewers here but I will not plagiarize. This really is a wonderful trash can and I can't believe I'm actually saying that. I guess I like it so much because I've only ever had trashcans that suck.
Everything about this trashcan is fantastic. There's a pocket for extra trash bags on the backside of the can. My little boy likes folding up trash bags and stuffing them into this pocket.
The foot pedal is well balanced with the trash can and when you step on it, the trash can doesn't lurch forward at you like other trash cans. The lid closes slowly so you can toss something extra in if you didn't remember the first time. The top rim lifts up and easily stands open so you can lift out a full trash bag and insert a new one. This is a terrific feature as most others have to be held open while you get the bag in only to have the lid slam down so hard that the bag falls into the can. NOT SO with this beauty. Now, it comes with simplehuman trash bags designed for this product that fit the trash can perfectly. They are lovely trash bags and durable as well. If you like higher quality trash bags, I recommend them, but they're not in my budget and so I buy the regular stretchy ones with the drawstring ties at the wholesale/bulk-item store nearest to me. The regular drawstring tie trash bags fit this can quite nicely. They are tight around the top rim of the can and I really like that because that means the they are NOT going to fall into the can no matter what I put in there. The store bags fit so well that we are able to stuff each bag so full that the bag will rip down lower but will never come loose at the top. This is not a problem for me. This trash can is easy to clean. Although this may not mean much, the stickers that came on the trash can were easy to remove too.
I think the lid closing slowly is probably my favorite feature because I had grown so tired of the slamming lids of other trash cans. I have three children and a husband and so the old trash can made a heck of a lot of noise for a very long time.
I'm so glad that I spent a little bit more and treated myself to this trash can. When people come to visit and compliment the trash can (can you believe that? that's how cool this thing is...) I can't help but tell them how much I like it.

top_profane.text:
[[VIDEOID:599dc46d5e2e3c521d5ccb9c85792a3e]] Overall, I really like my purchase. There are Tips, Pros, and Cons I want to list for other people thinking of buying this.
Tip: This purchase does not include a DC charger or D size batteries. Those are sold separate. You can choose either one, but you can’t use both at the same time for this trash can. In my opinion, 4 of the D size batteries are better than the DC adapter cable because I like to move my trash can with me throughout the house (I bought the Rolling trash can) and with the DC adapter, the trash can has to be plugged in for the Vision Sensor to work. Meaning, no plug, no sensor. I say Vision Sensor because as long as there’s something in front of the sensor, the trash can will open. You don’t have to move a lot for it to open, which brings me to the Pros and Cons.
Pros:
1. Very Convenient. I bought the rolling trash can so it can move with me. The sensor makes it so that I just I have to put my hand in front of it and it will open. No need to move around crazy for the sensor. I don’t have to touch it and it’s almost effortless! Even if my hands are full, if I stand in front of it, it will open. Love that!
2. Pet Lock. There’s a slider to stop the trash can from opening for just anything. Whether it’s a kid messing with the trash can or a pet that wants to go through the trash.
3. There’s a Odor Filter. I rarely have trash that smells bad. To be honest, I mostly use it to throw out paper, but the filter comes with the trash can, so that’s great for anyone that throws spoiled food or diapers.
4. The D size batteries last for 2 years.

Cons:
1. Trash Can is not “rechargeable.” If you use the AC adapter, it has to stay plugged in for the sensor on the trash can to work. Otherwise, it’s like any other trash can where you have to lift up the lid yourself, which is fine, but that defeats the purpose of buying a Vision Sensor trash can. Not going to lie, this disappointed me. I thought if I plug in my AC adapter for a period of time, it will “charge” and stay charged. Unfortunately, that is not the case.
2. The battery compartment for the D Size Batteries is under the lid, under the filter, and INSIDE the trash can. When I read this in the manual, this design did bother me because not only do you have to put the trash lid upside down to install batteries, it also bothers me to think that if someone was filling this trash can to the top with something that smells disgusting, that same disgusting trash will be touching where the batteries go. You can clean it, but I feel like the battery compartment should of been put on the outside of the lid, not the inside, and especially under a filter that you also have to take apart. A good spot for the battery compartment would have been next to where the input for the AC adapter was, which was on the back and outside of the trash can. I’m just saying. Again, I mostly use this for paper, so it’s not a problem for me. It will be for other people.
Just a reminder that these Pros and Cons are to help other buyers to decide if they want this trash can. In general, I am happy with my purchase. The design just wasn’t perfect.
top_profane.title: Very Convenient

top_profane.text:
This SimpleHuman 30L trash can is like the 6-inch stilettos of trash cans: Very attractive, but totally impractical. Yes, it's very easy on the eyes, with its brushed steel (fingerprint-resistant) exterior and clean, smooth lines. The slow-close lid seals the can shut, hiding your garbage out of sight (and containing some of its odor). It seems more like a modern art sculpture or piece of designer furniture than a waste disposal container. But at the end of the day, this is just a trash can - a very expensive one, but a trash can nonetheless. It's supposed to be a convenient place to temporarily store trash until you take it out for city/county pickup. Unfortunately, this trash can makes the last part of the equation exceedingly difficult. When you use the custom-sized "G" plastic bags, it takes two people and a TON of elbow-grease to remove the bag from the interior plastic liner can once it's full of trash. (Perhaps it's because of the static cling that causes the trash bag to stick to the interior plastic can). The can can only be loosely filled with trash, otherwise the bag is a hassle to remove, not to mention the bruised palms and aching wrists caused by your efforts to yank the bag out of the interior can. It's a terrible waste of money and plastic to have to throw out the trash when the bags are only two-thirds full. (Especially given how much more expensive the SimpleHuman custom-sized trash bags are than regular trash bags). Plus, the 30L trash can itself is already on the small side, so being limited to filling it up only 2/3 of the way means that, practically-speaking, it can't hold enough to be suitable for use as a main kitchen trash can. And since this SimpleHuman trash can is over fifty dollars more expensive than most regular kitchen trash cans, it seems like a real shame to use it merely as a glorified waste basket for light trash only. The folks at SimpleHuman need to go back to the drawing board and figure out how to make it easier to remove the full trash bags from the interior plastic liner can. If they can fix this one issue, this would be the perfect trash can.

Grade: C
top_profane.title: Such a hassle to get full trash bags out of this can!

I have an admission. I am a well educated frugal consumer but I bought a really, really expensive garbage can and I love it!! Probably one of the best kitchen purchases I have ever made!

I live in a family of five people. Our town has single can recycling (everything in one big bucket). Over the years we just got used to having a step trash can and then leaving the recycling on the counter. That eventually devolved into having a bag sit on top of the trash can with various amounts of recycling in it. It was ugly and painful.

Over the years I looked at various Simplehuman recycling solutions. I came close to purchasing on several occasions but I just could't do it. How could any trash can be worth the money?!! And the liners, oh the liners. How could I possibly put myself into a situation to buy expensive liners from the expensive trash can company. I mean really!

Well, then it happened. I finally got pissed off at the recycling sitting on top of the trash and the dog taking it around the house. I also hated having to move the bag to actually throw out trash. Then I found this trash can. In the past, I was always worried there wasn't enough trash or recycling room under the lid. This can had the largest recycling bin and trash bin I could find. I pulled the trigger. Tears dripped down my cheeks as I paid $200 for a recycling can. What the hell, I could always return it. I don't HAVE to use their liners, I could just tie a regular old 13 gallon liner on the trash side. Let's see...

The can came. I pulled it out of the box and put the sample set of H size liners in the convenient pocket on the back of the trash side. All of a sudden, no more clutter and enough room for trash!! The skies opened and the Angels sang. And I have to tell you, it's worth every penny!!! The can looks nice and performs well. The recycling bucket comes right out and I can walk it to the garage to throw it in our town bin for collection. The size bags slide right out, tie up and go to the regular trash can. Before I leave with the full bag I just pull a new liner through the nice slot and put it right where it belongs. Genius.

Oh - and about the liners. They work. They work really, really well and are so convenient in the pocket on the trash can that I spent the money to get a whole lot of them (better price that way - I am after all still a frugal guy). Once you get your can, register the warranty on the simplehuman site. They will send you a coupon for dollars off an order at their site ($5.00 if I remember correctly). Go to the simplehuman site and you can order 240 liners (a size I was unable to find anywhere else) with free shipping. Use your coupon and buy them.

You only live once, don't stare at trash or loose recycling! Just do it. You wont be sorry.
top_profane.title: Hi, my name is Tom and I bought a Simplehuman trash can

top_profane.text:
Got a dog that will get into the trash? My husband passed away, and he was much adored by my two Australian shepherds. Because I lived in such a remote location, I took up temporary residence on my son's farm in an apartment. My housekeeper remained at my main residence for three months, and my dogs loved her as well. The move to the farm was traumatic for them. They'd lost Dad and their beloved friend who had loved them so well. They became very aggressive about acting out, chewing on woodwork, tearing open overnight bags, and strewing garbage all over the place. I have one incredibly clever Aussie who can open any door he sets his mind to open, sometimes even when they are locked with a drop-down bolt. If I left them alone for even thirty minutes, I came home to kitchen garbage strewn from one end of the house to another.

To be fair, these dogs had never done things like this until grief assailed them. But I knew I needed something bulletproof for my kitchen garbage while they adjusted to a whole new world. I found this Simple Human trash can, with a lock-down lid. I figured my clever dog would figure it out. But when it arrived, I even needed a trial session to figure it out. Once I did, it was easy to unlock. Boy, did it ever take care of the strewn trash! My Simple Human trash can might have been knocked over and pushed into the living room when I came home, but not one trace of garbage was outside of it. All I had to do was put the garbage can back where it belonged. No mess on the floor, period, and the can has no sharp edges to damage anything when it's shoved by a doggie nose.

I stayed home all year, even so, making my necessary trips to town as brief as possible. My dogs needed one person who didn't leave, and they got over their grief in time. They had done nothing naughty for months. But then my housekeeper, dearly loved, unexpectedly found another job that better suited her, and not that long after, I had a prescheduled trip abroad to see my son. I knew it would hit my dogs hard when I suddenly disappeared and left them with a new caregiver, even though it had been over a year. And, oh, yes, they were naughty while I was away. I'm sure they felt frantic. The last anchor in their lives had vanished, and I was gone for a month. They'd been so good for so long that I didn't think to tell the new caregiver about the lock on the garbage can lid. She endured daily trash attacks while I was gone. When I got home, she and I made a trip into town together and left the dogs alone. Before we left, I locked the lid of this trash can. When we got home, it was pushed into another room, and my clever dog had figured out kind-of, sort-of how the lock worked. But all his efforts went for naught. The trash can will forever bear the marks of his teeth gouging the plastic and metal near the locking mechanism, but the trash can prevailed.

My evaluation of this trash can:

It holds nearly as much as a wider and more space-stealing trash can.
Regular tall kitchen garbage bags fit it nicely
It rolls around easily for sink-side tossing when you are peeling and dicing. The lid stands up for that, making it easy.
It wipes down easily on the outside to keep it looking great
The foot pedal lift works great for easy opening of the lid
It's very attractive, as trash cans go
It is stable and doesn't tip over unless a big dog tackles it
I think a Rottie or Pit Bull could manage, with enough time, to chew part of the lid off, but it would require some time, and for most dogs, it is impervious to teeth. (Mine will always bear barely noticeable scars, right around the slide lock because my dog is so smart, but the can still looks nice.)
My ending recommendation is that I've never owned a better, dog-proof trash can. I love it so much I even got one for my son.
top_profane.title: Dog proof and fabulous!!!

top_profane.text:
This thing is so pretty... and so expensive... but so pretty! I mean, who doesn't want a trash can that can sit in plain sight and not look like ... well, trash? But the price?? Can one justify that price? I really struggled with this and in the end, i reluctantly bought this trash can feeling great pain over my poor financial decision making but I justified it with the following:

1) My last Simple Human trash can has lasted at least six years of daily, 'main trash can' use and is still in use. No lie. Other family members have bought from cheaper brands from all over but mine has stood the test of time- it is functional and still looks good. It has now been adopted by my more frugal sibling after his off brand model rusted and smelled beyond usability.

2) I need a recycling trash can. I don't have the space to buy two trash cans for that purpose and I do not like the idea of built in trash cabinets even though I have one. The thought of having one central depot for both that is free standing AND looks good was incentive enough to shell an embarrassing amount of money for the convenience.

Was it a good decision?

Well, I am pretty certain (or hope) that this trash can will also last several non-rusty, non-smelly years and if it does, that alone will be worth my current embarrassment (or at least I tell myself it will). To justify the cost I need it to perform through 2030 at least. Here's hoping. I've padded those odds by not purchasing the sensor trash can as i haven't met one who's sensor didn't break in short order... I could not justify the cost/upgrade.

Unfortunately, I am also realizing that while this trash can does an okay job of holding both trash and recycling, it doesn't really do a great job of either. At least not for me. At this price point it really should. It may be 58L total but in reality can only do half of that for either. The actual openings are pretty narrow and can just barely fit a gallon jug of milk or juice width wise.

What that means is that this trash can fills out very quickly and when all is said and done, doesn't hold that much trash or recycling. One has to empty the can pretty frequently in a high traffic home ( 3 people who cook often). whatever plastic we are saving in recycling, we are probably negating by not using our trash bags to capacity. We have taken to flattening what we can prior to increase capacity, but at this price, should not have to.

Had I seen this can in person I probably wouldn't have purchased it... or i would have gone for the single compartment model and found another way to deal with my recycling. Unfortunately we were in the thick of quarantine and I needed a trash can... so there you go.

I would only recommend this trash can if you are a single individual or a family that does not generate much trash. Otherwise, the equally pretty single compartment one will likely be more functional (and is slightly cheaper). I'd really prefer to return/exchange this one for a single compartment model but me thinks its too late.
( plus I already threw out the equally pretty box it came in). Sadly I see a second trash can in my future.

Here's hoping this one lasts a decade.
top_profane.title: Jack of all trades- Master of none.

top_profane.text:
[[VIDEOID:a232576eec0cf443c70a68ce50752c8d]] As an adult that was well past the age that hanging a trash bag off a drawer was even remotely acceptable, and having no good place for my la croix and Diet Coke cans like the basic b I am. I finally set out to find a proper trash can.

I was LOSING MY MIND over finding a trash can that was up to my unjustly bourgeois standards but wouldn’t cost $40000000000000 dollars…looking at you Simplehuman… sidebar, why are trash cans so expensive? Like I’m in the wrong field, I need that TRASH CAN MONEY HONAAYYYY!

I digress… I was still annoyed at how much it cost but it was what I wanted: a cute, covered place for my trash, and cans/bottles! So I bowed down to to the machine that is consumerism and bought it! Boy oh boy am I happy I took a chance on this shiny hunk of metal and plastic!!

Pros:
I have never wiped finger prints off of it since I bought it months ago

Keeps gross smells in (for the most part) and my pup out

Well made, sturdy

Keeps my cans and trash organized

Lock feature works like a charm… unless you get pepper seeds in it and don’t notice until you’re making a review video… ahem

Step lever is sturdy, smooth close is Barry white smooth, and the bins are easy to take out!

Cons:

TLDR: easy to overload, therefore can’t use compostable trash bags. That’s more the eco trash bags’ problem though.

The trash bin is tall and and not super wide, comparatively. Which means it can be somewhat easy to overload, or shove trash in too tightly, which then makes it hard to pull the bag out and can causes the bag to rip.

So You can’t be totally lazy and just keep shoving the trash down and compacting it. I’d say low to moderate shoving down/laziness is okay.

Also due to the dimensions, heftier trash bags are needed, in my opinion. Because the diameter of the apex of the weight of the trash is smaller, and therefore creates more concentrated force…. Rather than shorter fatter trash cans that more evenly distribute the weight/pressure on the bag and causes less ripping. Think of trying to poking a hole with a needle versus a baseball bat….. ya know?

Which leads me to another con: I can’t use my eco friendly bags bc they are too thin, nor can I put more in the trash to cut down on the amount of bags I use, so I am bummed in that department bc if I can’t gloat about my compostable trash bags, who even am I? But really that’s the eco trash bags’ problem.

Alright so anyway…. Besides that last bit… 1000% recommend this cute, well built, bougie on a budget trash can!
(Even though it may or may not want to see the earth burn. Like… idk.. but I’m just sayin’! I think it has good intentions and has a good heart but definitely shady in that department. Would not trust at a Phish concert.)
top_profane.title: Worth the splurge! But also easy to overload in my opinion.

top_profane.text:
Quick Summary:

* Great value with a large and small trash can included
* Large trash can features a redesigned battery chamber that takes 3 x C batteries
* Small trash can takes 4 x D batteries in a compartment protected in the bottom
* Great oblong shape for tighter spaces

Detailed Review:

After purchasing the [[ASIN:B0031M9HJ4 Nine Stars DZT-50-9 Infrared Touchless Stainless Steel Trash Can, 13.2-Gallon]], I was disappointed to find that the battery compartment in two separate chambers for the D batteries began to rust. As we use this trash can in the kitchen, it's not uncommon to throw away table scraps or cutaways from vegetables, etc. The old battery compartment had two rather flimsy covers and the battery contacts consisted of push on contacts that rusted easily. That garbage can became non-functional and we now use it without the lid.

In the interim, I had also bought the very highly reviewed [[ASIN:B000EJVYTS iTouchless Deodorizer Touch-Free Sensor 13-Gallon Automatic Stainless-Steel Trash Can]]. We actually love that trash can too. It has a few design features, including an odor-free chamber that you can place a bag to absorb odors. However, that trash can is a square shape and although we really liked it for upstairs, we needed a narrower/wider oblong trash can for the kitchen.

With some hesistation, I ordered the trash can set here. Even if I had to replace it in two years, I figured the value with an included small trash can might make it worth it. I also hoped that the rusting battery compartment was just a fluke on the first one.

Immediately when I opened the new trash can, I noticed that the battery compartment had been completely redesigned. Not only did the large trash can now take only 3 C batteries, but it also was a single chamber with a very tight cover. I'm hopeful with this redesign that the rusting issue I had in the very similar looking old model is a thing of the past.

As for the small trash can, it interestingly takes 4 D batteries, but the battery chamber is held in the bottom beneath a plastic bucket that houses the trash. Moisture should definitely NOT be an issue for this small trash can.

Overall, I'm quite happy with the purchase and thrilled with the value. If you've ever eyed SimpleHuman trash cans and suffered sticker shock, this Nine Stars package is a great relief.
top_profane.title: Great value w/ 2 trash can, and REDESIGNED battery chamber

top_profane.text:
Wow. My partner and I struggled over trash duty for years — it *was* our least favorite household chore, until this can came along. Now, as the title states, I actually take the trash out now on a regular basis like a normal person without whining even a little bit.

Yes, the price is a bit high, especially for poor millennials who just want to make their lives a little easier, but if you hate trash duty too, this is definitely worth it (try to get one used if possible!).

I didn’t realize until recently that the garbage can we had for years was the reason we hated it so much.
It was a “fancy” one with a split down the middle to separate trash & recycling, and a motion detector to open and close the lid (it would “bite” if we didn’t throw things away fast enough).

In order to take the trash out of that one, we had to switch off the battery, completely remove the 10lb+ lid as well as the bag holder rims, set the filthy things down on the floor or countertop, and grip the bottom of the can with our feet in order to yank out the bags (which nearly always caught & ripped on the sharp edges of the narrow opening, causing garbage-juice-puddles). No thank you.

So obviously, I was absolutely losing my mind thinking “how do people do this regularly????” and my partner was losing HIS mind thinking “why won’t you take the trash out when I do the dishes and vacuum and wipe the counters and everything else to avoid exactly that?” So, I started looking for an alternative.

When I came across this trash can (45L stainless), I was put off by the high price tag, but I was pretty desperate, and the more I looked into it, the more tempting it became.

A slow-close pedal that crosses the entire bottom, no batteries necessary (and no biting)?
A narrow body to fit in our small space without having to turn it sideways?
A rim that not only beautifully hides & secures the edge of the bag, but LIFTS UP all the way and STAYS THERE while you remove your garbage?
And a freakin bag dispenser, full of nice bags upon arrival?
Ok, fine, I’ll give it a shot.

Well, guys, we’ve had this baby for about a month now and I haven’t complained about trash duty once.
In fact, I rave about the experience every time, much to the chagrin of my partner, who doesn’t have quite as much fun with the dishes now.

Taking the garbage out now is a (incredibly metaphorical) breath of fresh air compared to the trash-trash can we were dealing with before.
It’s just so dang intuitive — ALL kitchen trash cans should be this simple, well-made, nice-looking, and easy to operate.

Some other things that make it extra great:

- No matter how much velocity I use to step on the pedal, the light little lid does not bang into the wall, and neither does the can, even though it’s literally RIGHT up against the wall.

- It’s nice and light, but not so light that it lifts up when I pull out a full bag, so I don’t have to hold it down every time. It’s also smooth & roomy inside, so removing a full bag is easy — a simple lift rather than an aggressive yank (and there’s nothing for it to catch on!).

- I haven’t gotten through the bags that come in the dispenser yet (I think there are a whole 50 of them), but we’ve inspected it, happily concluding that our bulk Kroger 13Gal kitchen trash bags will fit in there just fine. It’s so convenient not having to go dig around under my kitchen sink to re-line it.

- There’s no holder-rim-thingy. I just have to roll the bag over the edges for a perfect fit and close the secondary lid, which holds it in place AND totally hides my sloppy relining job.

- It’s so clean! There aren’t any gaps or holders or weird spaces for crud to hide. Nothing gets gunked under the lid, nothing bounces out, the bags don’t catch & tear allowing garbage juice to pool on the bottom.

I strongly believe it’s worth making adjustments that’ll improve quality of life, even a little. This was well worth the $120-ish to neutralize the trash duty struggle. We’ll be likely to get another one or two of these when we move to a larger living space.

Note: nobody asked me to write this, I’m just really enthusiastic to be relieved of this particular anxiety with such a simple solution. :)
top_profane.title: I actually take out the trash now

top_profane.text:
For years, I had the idea to install two pull out trash cans in my kitchen, one for trash and one for recycling. But my two available cupboard openings were only 9" wide, and whenever I researched this (multiple times, hours on end, over the years), it seemed all the ready-made solutions wanted at LEAST 10" wide and even then they had very small trash cans. It looked to me like it should be possible to have a pull out trash that just barely cleared the sides & top and still was pretty big, but no one made it, or I could not find it.

Finally, I decided this HAD to happen, and I'd either find a product or get it custom made. At that time, my search found this Knape & Vogt 35qt product. It called for 9 5/8", I think. However, though my framed opening was only 9", the interior width of my cabinets are 10". Would it work? I decided to gamble, and bought one of these to try.

Initially, I was shocked how easy this was to install. I thought the rails would be separate, and I'd have to mount them on the sides of the cabinetry, and it would be tricky to align & mount. However, the way both rails are mounted together on a "sled" that mounds to the bottom, it takes literally 2 minutes to install the whole thing! And it seems very stable.

In fact, the two rails-sled contraption is about 8 7/8" wide (maybe 8 15/16"?) at its widest, so it fit right in to my space. The only thing that did NOT fit was the very top of the trash can, as it gets larger as it gets higher, and then the rim of the trash has some plastic "lip" that curls out maybe 3/8 of an inch. So I wound up chiseling / sanding just that 1 inch part of the frame where the top of the trash can was rubbing. about 1/4 inch outward per side, and it slides in. The door of the cabinet is still wider than the enlarged opening, so this mutilated frame does not show at all unless the cabinet is open, and even then it is basically hidden by the trash bag itself (see pics). (It occurred to me when I was over halfway done that maybe I should have instead trimmed the trash can "lip" off instead of gouging my kitchen cabinetry, but oh, well, I'm not really sure which solution is better.)

I also opted for the additional hardware to mount the door to the frame, for true "pull out" trash/recycling (otherwise, what's the point?) and I'm so glad I did, makes it SOOO easy. However, be warned that the mounting of the door on the frame is MUCH harder than mounting the pull out system itself. I had to look up words from the instructions in a friggin' dictionary, and I was still utterly confused. I actually wound up NOT USING the two metal plates that came with that product, and just used the two brackets that came with it, screwed directly into the door, and it seems very stable. The "holes" in the brackets are ovals that let you raise or lower before you tighten to "fine tune" the location. I was worried that the force of the screw heads pushing the bracket into the door might not be strong enough to hold the door in place over time if I had it raised a bit from the lowest position, so I added small washers to create a wider pressure area, and it seems very stable to me. From the pics, you can see I think I got these lined up pretty darn well, and I'm not handy at all.

I did not use any drill for this. However, the screws they provided are not the type that easily screw into wood without a starter hole (a bit too thick, which is a good thing for strength). The screws I had from removing the cupboard doors were thinner, and easily screwed into wood with no starter hole, so I used one of those to start the holes, then unscrewed them and used the provided screws since they seem thicker/stronger, and that worked fine.

I had some extra rubber "tool chest liner" that I cut to fit the floor of the cabinetry (10" x 22"?) and I laid it down on the cabinet floor before I mounted the bottom rail system (no glue or anything, just relying on the screws holding the rail system to also hold down the rubber liner), so that any spill/mess will be on wipe-able rubber, not on the wood, and I do recommend this. It looks much cleaner this way.

I've seen some pull out trash that uses the inner area behind the trash can to hold stuff. This system has nothing there to hold anything. However, I had a small metal basket and I tied it to the back of the trash-holder frame with some metal wire and put my extra trash bags in there. So you CAN make use of that space if you are a bit crafty/creative (about 8-9" deep I think).

I only bought one of these and mounted it for the trash, but once I saw it was working, I bought a second and did the recycling side. I have not yet moved the handles on the doors to the middle position. As it is now, you cannot tell these are not regular cupboards when closed, and they open pretty easily, but I still might center them & turn them like drawer pulls to make it a bit easier. I'm using 8 gallon large trash cans for these, which seems very large to me compared to what I was using. It makes cleaning up so easy. I wish I'd done this years ago.
top_profane.title: So good, why did I wait so long???

top_profane.text:
TL;DR: Too tall so standard trash bags fall in; the protective rim around the bottom fell off after a month; too narrow so you can't fit many normal pieces of trash (like take-out boxes) in it; Odor protection is okay but not great; motion detection is okay, but less functional in dark spaces.

Okay, I had been wanting to get a decent motion detector trash can for ages, and we were moving in to our new home, so I was ready to bit the bullet. It got to my house, and I unpacked it and it was pretty simple to put together. I put the first trash bag in, and plopped a small (but a little heavy) piece of garbage in it. Oof--the standard sized trash bag doesn't reach to the bottom, and the whole thing fell in. I thought--that will be okay; once I secure the lid in place it will hold it up. My illusions were shattered after a few more pieces of trash--the lid was not tight enough to hold the bag up. Why on earth would a 13 gallon trash can not hold a 13 gallon trash bag?!
That was annoying, but it wasn't a deal breaker. We just started taking the trash out a little more often, before it was so heavy that it made the bag fall. I put the can in the chosen spot and we started using it consistently for a while. We noticed another problem due to the chosen location (also the only reasonable location for the trash can in the house--the laundry cupboard behind the kitchen). The new problem was that the motion sensor didn't always work in the dark. Sometimes it would after a couple of passes--but most of the time I had to turn on the cupboard light to get the sensor to see me. Also not a deal breaker, but very annoying, and defeats the purpose of a motion-sensor trash can.
Then, we started being frustrated by how narrow the opening of the can is. We couldn't fit some of our regular trash in due to the size. We ended up having a separate (uncanned) trash bag for things like take-out boxes, bulky drink containers (non-recyclable ones), standard packaging, etc. Okay--so why do I want to have a trash can that won't contain my trash? Well, I don't.
On top of that, the narrow size meant that when the bag was full, pulling it out of the can was really difficult--sometimes it caused the bag to tear, and we had to get a fresh bag to contain that torn bag. Not convenient or economical.
So, all in all, this trash can might be good in a well-lit small space in a home where they don't use trash over 12 inches wide or 16 ounces in weight. But here, in the land of take out and energy savings, it's just not okay.
top_profane.title: I wanted to like it, but there are too many design flaws

top_profane.text:
Let me get straight to the point. This is a great-looking, great-quality, dent-free Trash bin on wheels (wheels are optional to use). I've been in my home for one year and had my Contractor's garbage bags on the floor because I couldn't find a Trash Bin big and nice-looking enough to fit my bags. I finally came across Seville Trash Bin and I'm so happy I purchased. My Contractor's garbage bags are a little too big for this Trash bin, but this is the only bin that came close to fitting my bags. I push my garbage bag a little further down into the Bin and it solves the problem of bags being too big. Simple!!

I didn't want a sensor trash bin because they tend to eventually stop working. I didn't want a trash bin with an annoying flap and neither did I want to have to step on a pedal that sometimes stick which causes the garbage top to stay open. Seville Trash bin is free of all those annoying instances. Garbage bags go in with no problem and comes out just as easy. It's a smooth finish inside, which makes it easy to pull bags out. There's no fighting or tearing of garbage bags. Even though the picture shows a smooth finish on the Trash bin, it's not. It actually has ribbed lines going across, which gives it a really nice look.

The top of the Trash Bin is big enough to actually rest something on it. I had two plates in my hand and rested one on top of the Trash Bin while I emptied the other. The hole is big enough not to spill garbage on the floor, and it's easy to take the top off and put back on. As I mentioned earlier, the wheels are optional. I didn't put the wheels on my Bin because I placed the Trash Bin in my hallway right outside of my kitchen area. My hallway has Bamboo flooring (softer than hard-wood). I don't want to take any chances of having tiny wheel dents on the floor. I'm not saying this WILL happen, I just don't want to take any chances. There's no need for me to wheel the Bin back and forth from one area to another. I like it stationary.

I'm actually contemplating purchasing another Bin for my recyclables. It would be nice if Seville made a blue top/cover for Recyclable items. Just a thought :-)
